title: Lily Peck
text:
In review


De Carvalho C, Ord J, Escuer P, Fox J, Li-Byarlay H, Peck LD, Planidin N, Venney C, Marshall H (2025). Long-read sequencing for epigenomic studies in the fields of ecology and evolution. Cell Genomics. https://zenodo.org/records/14671205


Accepted


Sork VL, Buck RC, Peck LD & Wright JW. Accepted at Proceedings of the 8th California Oak Symposium. Sustaining Oak Woodlands under Current and Future Conditions. U.S. Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Pacific Southwest Research Station. 21st century tools for conserving oak populations under threat of climate change.


Published


Peck LD, Llewellyn T, Bennetot B, O’Donnell S, Nowell RW, Ryan MJ, Flood J, Rodriguez de la Vega R, Ropars J, Giraud T, Spanu PD, Barraclough TG (2024). Horizontal transfers between fungal Fusarium species contributed to successive outbreaks of coffee wilt disease. PLoS Biology 22 (12): e3002480

title: News
text:
Autumn 2024: New paper published in PLOS Biology which uses historic strains of the coffee wilt disease pathogen to show that host specificity and disease were driven by serial horizontal transfer events. All analyses, scripts, and code are on .
Autumn 2024: New opinion article published in Trends in Plant Science in which we argue that, as in crop plants, some types of DNA methylation in trees can induce phenotypic changes, including changes in climate-relevant traits. This follows recent findings in a few tree species that show phenotypic change following DNA methylation of promoter regions upstream of genes.
Summer 2024: Gave a talk in the Adaptive Epigenetics session at the 3rd Joint Congress on Evolutionary Biology meeting in Montréal: “Can DNA methylation help us understand climate adaptation in trees?”
Summer 2024: The Lake Wildfire erupted in the Santa Ynez Valley, where the UC Sedgwick Reserve is located, a long-term oak study site for the Sork lab. We went up as a lab the week following the fire, and I returned after a month to measure physiological traits of burnt and un-burnt trees… watch this space!

title: Lily Peck
text: I’m an evolutionary biologist with broad interests in the ecology and adaptation of natural populations of plants and fungi. I use whole-genome sequence data along with population genomics and comparative phylogenetics to better understand how these processes contribute to evolutionary change. I’m currently a postdoctoral scholar at the University of California Los Angeles, Ecology and Evolutionary Biology Dept. working with Prof. Victoria Sork. Specifically, I am currently working on genetic and epigenetic responses of tree populations to climate change. I received my PhD from Imperial College London in the Barraclough lab, where I used cryogenically preserved living isolates to track fungal evolution over a timescale of decades. Click through the tabs for more information about the projects and publications which I am involved with.

title: Research
text:
Response to environmental stress

Many plant and animal species are suffering in response to a rapidly changing climate. Here in California, over 10 million trees (or 20% of total forest cover) experienced mass die off between 2013-15 in response to historic drought and wildfire. This environmental crisis necessitates a basic understanding of the biological processes underlying both short-term and long-term (or evolutionary) responses to these new climates. For most locally adapted populations, the reaction to a novel climate condition, such as drought, will be changes in expression of stress-related and drought-related genes. My work in the Sork lab as a postdoc is analysing the biological processes related to short- and long-term environmental stress response. I am using seedlings from a California endemic oak to explore the role of genetics, epigenetics, regulatory mechanisms and physiological response at a molecular and population scale.

Mechanisms of evolutionary change

Across eukaryota, we see dizzying amounts of diversity, with different organisms undergoing peculiar mechanisms through the processes of adaptation and diversification. I am particularly interested in how genome structure interacts with these processes in complex non-model organisms. My PhD focussed upon the emergence and divergence of a fungal plant pathogen, finding that horizontal gene transfer from a distant relative occurred repeatedly and could be one of the drivers behind the repeated emergence of coffee wilt disease over the last century via the transfer of pathogenicity gene- and repeat-rich genomic regions. My postdoc is quantifying dramatic differential DNA methylation, a type of epigenetic change, in response to environmental stress in oaks, and how this shapes expression of drought-responsive genes and physiological phenotypes. Such mechanisms, be it horizontal transfer or epigenetic modifications, can be beneficial in increasing genome variability, which in turn can result in the evolution or activation of stress-responsive genes. Such processes can also result in adaptation to new hosts or new environments and subsequent speciation.

Plant-pathogen dynamics
